title: A Mehxico Story
body:
Mexico had the honour of opening the festival of football and, in true Roman style, they needed a sacrifice. The hapless lambs were South Africa. Every World Cup I find myself wondering whether this will be the year African nations finally show their strength and depth. Unfortunately, if South Africa are anything to go by, the answer remains a firm no.

They looked out of their depth from the first whistle and Mexico should have been several up by half time. Against better opposition, El Tri might have rued those missed chances — but South Africa never really looked like making a game of it. A second goal in the second half, bookended by a flurry of red cards (three in total, a World Cup opening game record), made the second period watchable if not exactly a classic. Mexico cruised.

title: Good Korea, Bad Korea
body:
I only caught the highlights of the day's second game — which may become something of a recurring theme — but it looked like another dominant first half from the nominal home side. South Korea racked up almost as many missed chances as there were empty seats in Guadalajara (also likely to be a recurring theme), and went in at the break with nothing to show for it.

The second half started brightly for South Korea, but Czech Republic somehow fashioned a goal against the run of play. Luckily for those of you with the good Korea in your picks, they levelled things up before substitute Oh Hyeon-gyu popped up to secure the winner in the closing stages. A Soucek header that briefly threatened to nick it back for the Czechs was ruled out for offside, and that was your lot.

title: No They Can'tada
body:
Day two saw our remaining hosts get their tournaments underway with mixed results.

Canada went behind early and spent the rest of the game trying to claw it back. Throughout, they looked the side more intent on actually playing football, while Bosnia and Herzegovina were perfectly happy to sit back, soak up pressure, and wait for something to happen on the break. It was exactly one of those breaks that saw them burst clear and take the lead.

Canada continued to push, but their waves of attack kept crashing against a wall of defenders and rarely troubled the opposition's box.

The second half was more of the same — Canada creating, BiH sneaking. Canada's equaliser with ten minutes remaining felt like it would set up a breathless finale, but although both sides had chances, a draw felt like the right result after an entertaining match.

title: Surprisingly Fine
body:
South of the (Canadian) border, in the hallowed land of Soccer, the USA started with high expectations — and after this display, those expectations might actually be warranted. Their first half against Paraguay was some of the best football I've seen from the US team, and their 3–0 lead at the break felt thoroughly deserved.

Their opener — seven minutes in — was a horrible poke-in from a Paraguay defender, but it came at the end of some lovely build-up play. By the time Balogun added his second deep into first-half injury time, the US could stroll into the dressing room feeling very pleased with themselves.

The second half wasn't as convincing. The US eased off, made a few more mistakes, and some frailties at the back emerged that a better team might look to exploit. Paraguay did find the net with a well-worked goal the US defence should have dealt with more comfortably — but it was only a small speed bump on the road to a well-won victory. A fourth goal just before the final whistle was the bow on top.
footer: A personal footnote: while watching the 2002 World Cup with an American girl I was trying to impress, I made the drunken prediction that the US would reach a World Cup final within 25 years. This is — somewhat alarmingly — that window's last chance. I don't think it's going to happen. But they do look closer than I've seen them before.

title: A Match Worth Waiting For?
body:
As the media has frequently reminded us, it's been 28 years since Scotland made it to a World Cup. For Haiti the wait has been twice as long, and from the first whistle they were keen to make clear they hadn't travelled all this way just to be a footnote.

Every chance they got, Haiti came flying forward in numbers, with Scotland working hard to hold off the cavalry charge.

Scotland had their chances — not as many as they'd have liked — and it ultimately came down to a scuffed McGinn shot that deflected past the keeper to put them ahead. A goal on the board settled Scotland's nerves somewhat, though Haiti continued to threaten an equaliser right to the end.

Desperate for a cushion, Scotland probed for a second, but poor finishing and dogged defending kept Haiti in it until the final whistle.

Three points from their opening game was the bare minimum Scotland needed if they're to have any hope of reaching the knockouts. Morocco and Brazil are considerably sterner tests, and they're going to need to play a good deal better than this to squeeze out results that could get them through the group stage.

title: Scoring Is All That Matters
body:
Australia are one of those sides that always seems to promise a lot at World Cups and rarely delivers. Turkey were one of the teams I thought might outperform their PickerGame cost. We've established what I know.

Turkey applied pressure for 90 minutes. They had most of the ball, most of the chances... and Australia scored twice. And that, as they say, is how you take the points.

In a game that will not trouble the history books, a couple of individual moments separated two sides unlikely to trouble the sharp end of the knockout stages. Turkey will need to be more ruthless in front of goal. Australia will need to do, well, more — but they'll take it.

title: A Close Shave
body:
When I looked at the match schedule, the first fixture that leapt out at me was Brazil vs Morocco. A meeting between arguably the best sides from South America and Africa — hopefully a feast of football.

Out of the blocks it was Morocco who looked the sharper side. They had several chances and it was no surprise when a great through ball and a deft dink put them one up.

Going behind gave Brazil a nudge, though they still needed a moment of individual brilliance from Vinícius Jr. to claw their way back into it.

Brazil would have been relieved to go in level at the break, but any hopes of a change in fortune in the second half were swiftly dashed. Both sides made changes, Morocco continued to look the better team, and they'll feel they deserved more than a point — though a draw against the tournament favourites is a decent return. Brazil will be sending Vini Jr. and Alisson a very large fruit basket and quietly hoping the rest of the squad turns up for the next one.

title: Persistence Pays
body:
I've been saying in the build-up to this World Cup that Qatar would do better than expected. Well, even a broken cuckoo clock is right twice a day.

Not taking your chances has been a theme of this tournament already. While Canada had to settle for a point, Switzerland drawing with Qatar is the first genuine upset of the competition.

The Swiss had already squandered several clear chances before being awarded a penalty that wasn't — or at least shouldn't have been. It was well struck, but I'm going to need more proof it wasn't offside before I move on.

Switzerland continued to spurn chances for the remainder of the game, always looking the better side but never quite looking like they actually wanted to win it.

What Qatar lacked in talent they made up for in sheer bloody-mindedness — though it never really felt like it would pay off. When Lee Dixon said, two minutes into injury time, that there was still time for Qatar to grab a point, I filed it under "things pundits say." Reader, he was right.

With less than two minutes of injury time remaining, Qatar were on the board and seconds away from their first ever World Cup point. The Swiss finally found second gear. Too little, too late.

title: One Nil to the Team That Scored
body:
This was a game between two teams who felt defending was beneath them and shots on target were against the spirit of the game. For the majority of the ninety minutes both sides seemed perfectly happy to break with pace and then put the final shot wide.

Ecuador started the stronger but Ivory Coast soon found their way into the game.

One goal was always going to be enough to steal the points, and in the end it was Ivory Coast who managed to find the net — though "find the net" perhaps flatters both teams given the afternoon they'd had.

title: A View From The Sofa (Highlights Only)
body:
I turned on my TV at 8pm this evening, ready to watch Germany humble one of the many teams who should probably be at home watching. Thanks to the frankly baffling match schedule, all I got was adverts followed by the build-up to the next game.

So this is another match report based on ten minutes of highlights. Unlike the middle-of-the-night games — which no one watches and I can embellish freely — I have to be at least vaguely careful not to just make things up with this one.

This was always going to be one-sided, and when Germany went one up from a great strike six minutes in, expectations were duly set.
When Curaçao equalised, it felt like an M. Night Shyamalan twist — although much earlier in the story than usual. Were we about to see a repeat of Germany's group stage collapses from the last two World Cups?

Unfortunately for the tiny island nation, the celebrations for their first ever World Cup goal weren't destined to last. Germany were back on script with two goals before half time and another within minutes of the restart.

As the goals continued to flow, Curaçao fashioned a few chances but were never really going to get back into it. Hopefully, they can add to their tally before what looks likely to be a brief tournament run comes to an end.

For Germany, things can only get harder — but they'll be glad to have started strong and look every inch one of the favourites to be there when it matters.

title: Shower Timing: A Cautionary Tale
body:
The first 50 minutes of this clash weren't the most riveting football of the tournament. As it was getting late I decided to quickly jump in the shower before catching the last thirty minutes. Unfortunately this meant I missed all three goals.

The thing I find most surprising about the hydration breaks is that we just get to watch players drinking water. Surely they could at least show us a mini highlights package. Or failing that, some adverts. At least then I'd know what car I should be driving.

Netherlands are one of those teams who seem like they should be a footballing giant, but who always end up underperforming. Like Chelsea. Japan feel like they should be the pride of Asian football, but I'm not sure where that pride should rank in the world game.
Both teams should make it through to the knockouts but will be hoping for better performances.

Ok, I'll admit it. I was keen to get to bed so wrote this with 15 minutes left on the clock hoping for an easy life. Japan deserved that equaliser and hopefully that's the last time I need to update this.

title: Flat Pack Football
body:
Sweden are one of those teams I feel should do well but never quite expect to reach the semifinals. Tunisia I'll confess I know nothing about, so I genuinely can't tell you whether this scoreline was a shock or whether Sweden simply played a blinder.

Tunisia had some good attacks and deserved to get on the scoresheet, but even at 2-1 this never really looked like finishing in anything other than a Swedish win. Their front line looks sharp and I think they're going to punish any team that can't get their defence in order.

This was always going to be a tough group and I suspect Tunisia will struggle to pick up much from their remaining games.

title: A Point Each, Nobody Happy
body:
Uruguay were quick out of the blocks in a game they would have expected to win comfortably. Saudi Arabia sat deep and absorbed the pressure, though they were still grateful for the acrobatic reflexes of their keeper, who single-handedly kept them in the contest during the opening exchanges.

Saudi Arabia offered little going forward in the first half, but it was from one of their rare forays that they earned the corner, which, against the run of play, put them ahead.

The goal lit a fire under Uruguay, who turned the pressure up even higher. For a long period it looked like they'd run out of ideas, but they eventually forced the ball over the line with around ten minutes remaining.

Uruguay will be frustrated they couldn't convert their dominance into more. Saudi will be disappointed their dam was eventually breached after holding firm for so long. On balance, a draw was probably fair, though it'll feel different in each dressing room.

title: Cape Fear
body:
Just 10% of the way through the tournament and I think we've already seen the shock of the competition. Spain were my favourites to lift the trophy next month, but a resolute defensive display earned Cape Verde a point that will be remembered for years.

Spain were languid — and although they spent much of the game camped on the edge of the Cape Verde penalty area, truly threatening forays into the box were few and far between. Even the introduction of their hero in waiting couldn't make enough of a difference.

That's not to say Cape Verde's keeper didn't pull off some impressive saves to keep his side in it — he did, and deserves enormous credit.

Spain will need to do considerably better, though qualifying for the next round should still be comfortable enough. Cape Verde will look at their upcoming fixture against Saudi Arabia and quietly start dreaming about sticking around in this competition longer than anyone expected.

title: Iran, NZ Ran, Everyone Ran
body:
Iran started fast and with intent, and for the opening exchanges the New Zealand defence looked like it might buckle at any moment. Lines were disorganised, clearances were nervous, and Iran smelled blood. Which made it all the more against the run of play when New Zealand got their name on the scoresheet first, a well-worked move that Chris Wood was instrumental in, finished coolly by a just who was alive to the opportunity.

The goal did little to settle either defence, and both sides continued to look generous at the back. Iran, to their credit, kept pressing and were rewarded just before half time with a deserved equaliser.

The second half followed much the same script. New Zealand retook the lead, Iran pegged them back, and for the final stretch both teams went in search of a winner, with chances at both ends deep into injury time keeping everyone on their feet.

Both teams showed some genuinely nice build-up play and constructed their goals well, though it's always easier to look good in attack when the opposition's defence is handing out time and space like free samples.

title: Belgian Waffle
body:
Belgium have been at or near the top of the world rankings for a decade, and not once in that time have I heard anyone say they were excited to watch them play or that they were favourites to win a tournament.
So although I went into this game expecting a Belgium win, I wasn't entirely surprised when Egypt went one up with a brilliant driven shot.

Egypt should have had a second midway through the second half, but it was Belgium who ended up with the next name on the scoresheet — and from that point the game never quite recovered its earlier spark.

Both teams had chances, but Egypt will probably feel the most hard done by with a draw.

title: Mmmbappé
body:
I spent much of half time writing this report in my head. I was going to talk about how little interest the French team had in defending, how Mbappé was looking past his best, how Senegal looked dynamic going forward while France looked like they'd rather be somewhere else entirely.

Then, ten minutes into the second half, the game came alive. The French attacking line woke up as if someone had flicked a switch, and the confidence the Senegal defence had shown for forty-five minutes quietly evaporated.

Chances came at both ends, but it was France who got on the scoresheet first, despite having a clear penalty denied even after the referee took a long, presumably educational, trip to the monitor. A brilliant counter-attack with ten minutes remaining put France two up and on cruise control.

By ninety minutes it felt like the result was settled. Then, in the 95th minute, Senegal pulled one back with a wonder strike and suddenly the possibility of something extraordinary raised its head.

That head was swiftly removed, less than a minute later, by another potential goal of the tournament from Mbappé.

One of the best games of the tournament so far ended with a win for one of the favourites. Senegal will rue their missed chances in the first half, which could have killed the game off before France had even laced their boots. France will hope they can sustain the pace and intensity of that second half, and if they can, they'll expect to be very much in the mix come the semi-finals.

title: The Haaland Show (And Supporting Cast)
body:
Today was a day for the superstars to shine, and Erling Haaland was handed his first opportunity to make his case for World Cup glory.

An exciting first half offered chances for both teams, but it was Haaland who struck first. He's the kind of player who seems to force goals into existence through sheer strength and willpower rather than coaxing them gently, but it works, and it's genuinely thrilling to watch.

Iraq were never going to let him have it all his own way, and their equaliser was thoroughly deserved. Unfortunately for them, a loose back pass to the keeper was all the encouragement Haaland needed to pounce and restore Norway's lead before the break.

In the second half Norway looked more settled and cohesive, and Iraq,  despite having their moments,  never truly looked like taking anything from the game. Norway's third and fourth goals were the clearest sign yet that this is not simply a one-man show. There is a team here that could go deep into this competition.

title: The Conductor
body:
Coming into this World Cup, I wondered whether it might be a tournament too far for the game's megastars. Would the names that all the world knows struggle to keep pace with fresher faces and younger legs?

Tonight suggested there is still plenty of life in some of the old dogs.

Let's not pretend otherwise, this was the Messi show. He was the conductor, coaxing a symphony out of the Argentine orchestra around him.

In the first half Algeria looked like they might make a game of it. They had the greater number and higher quality of chances, but Argentina had Messi, and over and over again he proved the difference. His first goal, a long-range strike the keeper will feel he should have done better with, was one of Argentina's few bright moments of the opening half. In the second half they found their rhythm, and although Algeria continued to threaten, the result never truly felt in doubt. It was simply a matter of when Messi scored next, rather than if.

His first World Cup hat trick is a statement, and a timely response to Mbappé's earlier performance this evening.

title: Weird Balls
body:
I don't know if it's something to do with the World Cup ball, but we've had a remarkable number of great long-range goals so far this tournament. It was one of those strikes that put Austria ahead against the run of play.

Despite having a bucketful of chances throughout the first half, it wasn't until the 50th minute that Jordan were able to score a thoroughly deserved equaliser, and for a spell it looked like they might go on and nick something from the game.

Unfortunately, no matter how well they played, Jordan were destined to leave empty-handed. An own goal and a very late penalty gave Austria all three points and sent Jordan home disappointed, probably wondering what might have been had they taken even a handful of those first-half chances.

title: Highlights? What Highlights?
body:
You can't always tell how good a match has been from the highlights. I once tried to explain to my wife, who grew up on baseball, that football isn't always about the goals. That a tense, hard-fought 0-0 could be just as exciting as a 4-3 ding-dong. I may have been exaggerating slightly, and it didn't work. She still can't stand it.

Watching the highlights of this match, my overwhelming impression was that neither side is going to trouble anyone in this tournament. Panama looked the stronger of the two, but only marginally, and never looked like fashioning anything from their attacks. Ghana didn't register a shot on target until the 50th minute, and I'm not sure they managed a second until deep into injury time.

I was fully expecting a goalless draw, which would have made progression extremely difficult for both teams, when in the 95th minute Ghana surprised me, and I'm guessing most people watching the highlights, with a tap-in from a well-placed cross. You could argue this was against the run of play, but that would imply there was any meaningful play at the other end to speak of.

The goal seemed to wake Panama up, and the final couple of minutes featured a few frantic half-chances that came to nothing.

Both teams will struggle to pick up much from their remaining fixtures. One of them might sneak through to the knockouts, but I'd be surprised if either goes any further than that.

title: Emphatic on Paper
body:
I was never that good at football, and no one has ever asked me to coach the England team. But if they ever do, my first act will be to fine anyone who attempts a stutter run-up for a penalty. Any novelty penalty technique, frankly. Zero tolerance.

Luckily Harry Kane was given a second chance to do the right thing, and ten minutes into their World Cup campaign England were on the scoresheet.

England looked considerably better going forward than at the back, and Croatia were quick to identify and pounce on that frailty to level things up.

Both sides continued to have chances, and it was Kane again whose powerful header put England back in front. Any hopes of going into the break with that lead intact were dashed right on the whistle by a brilliantly worked piece of Croatian team play that brought the game level again.

Jude Bellingham must have had a few choice words at half time, because he came out of the dressing room like a bullet. England were back on top within two minutes, and he should have had a second barely a minute later.
For the next ten minutes we saw the England we always knew they could be. Intense, relentless pressure that somehow Croatia managed to weather without conceding.
There was no way England could sustain that level, and as the second hydration break approached, Croatia began to push back. Into the unofficial fourth quarter, England started to look shakier. Croatia struggled to win possession but pressed forward, stifling England's attempts to play out from the back.

It wasn't until the 85th minute that England finally put the game to bed. A brilliant move saw substitutes Saka and Rashford combine to make it 4-2, and that was that.

It's a great result, but it's done little to settle my nerves about the defence. They looked like a back line still getting to know each other, which is a fairly damning indictment of Tuchel's insistence on rotating his options right up until the final training session. You pick a defence, you stick with it. Apparently not.

A win is a win, and on paper this looks emphatic. But the second half performance only papered over the cracks that were so visible in the first.

title: Congo-ing Nowhere
body:
Those of you who are PickerGame veterans may remember that I am not a member of the Ronaldo fan club. There are plenty of reasons, but it's probably easiest to just accept it as fact and move on. So when Portugal took an early lead, I was glad it wasn't He Who Should Not Be Playing on the scoresheet, but also quietly worried that this Portugal side might actually be quite good.

Luckily, those fears proved unfounded. That early goal was one of the few clear chances they'd find themselves with over the full ninety minutes.

DR Congo (does anyone else find it sounds genuinely awkward every time a commentator says that) had a number of chances throughout, but it wasn't until the last kick of the first half that they managed to find the back of the net.

In the second half Portugal had the majority of possession but never looked capable of regaining the lead. Ronaldo seemed content to stand around waiting for someone to roll the ball into his path, and his mere presence appeared to throw the rest of the team off balance, which is quite an achievement when you think about it.

Another game where neither team deserved to win, and neither will be happy with the draw or their performance.

title: Welcome to the World Cup, Uzbekistan
body:
It might be down to the editing, but from the highlights this match looked considerably more exciting than the Ghana vs Panama game earlier this morning. Alternatively, it could just be that there were actual goals.

Colombia were out of the blocks fast, and Uzbekistan, one of the tournament's debutants, looked like they might struggle to stay in it.

It took until the 40th minute for Colombia to get on the scoresheet, but rather than killing the game off, the goal acted as a wake-up call to the Uzbeks. When they levelled in the 60th minute, it didn't feel like they'd go on to take all three points, but at least they were making a game of it.

A goalkeeping error let Luis Diaz put Colombia back in front, and it felt deserved. A third goal in the 99th minute made the result look a good deal more comfortable than it had actually been.

title: Czech, Please
body:
I don't think there is a team in this tournament who can look less like they're going to win a game when they're 1-0 up than Czech Republic. Like a cat up a tree, they keep finding themselves in the lead with absolutely no idea how to get down again.

At no point in the game did South Africa look like they were going to salvage anything from this match, right up until the moment they did.

Neither team deserved any points. Unfortunately, that's not how football works. Or Pickergame.

title: Switzerland, Eventually
body:
For much of this game it felt like a slog. I know that's hard to believe from the scoreline, but scores can be deceiving.

Switzerland were clearly the better side and had the majority of the chances. BiH had a few breaks where they nearly fashioned something, but for most of the game it was the Swiss doing the pushing. For 73 minutes their attacks crashed upon a defensive wall, and on the rare occasions they did get through, the keeper was there to make the stop.

After the 74th minute, everything went a bit crazy.
Switzerland were finally rewarded for their persistence with a goal, which only encouraged them to push for more. Five minutes later, a reckless challenge on a Swiss player clean through on goal brought out the referee's red card, and any hope Bosnia had of clawing things back walked off the pitch and straight back into the dressing room.

A second and third goal followed in short order, and that felt like that. Until, four minutes into injury time, Bosnia found the back of the net with a sweet strike from the edge of the box. With barely a minute left, this should have been the footnote that ended my report. But another reckless Bosnian challenge, this time in the box and admittedly with minimal contact, had the referee pointing to the spot. Switzerland stepped up, scored, and reclaimed their thoroughly deserved three-goal margin.

title: O Canada
body:
After their first game results, I thought this might be a close one. Sometimes I'm wrong.

From early on the Qatari defence had no idea how to deal with the crosses Canada were flinging into the box, and the Canadians were absolutely determined to make them pay. They were dogged in chasing down every loose ball and parried shot, and it was one of those dropped saves that let them sneak the opener.

A second soon followed, and at that point the result felt sealed. When Canada thought they had a penalty the crowd were ready to celebrate a memorable 3-0, but after a lengthy VAR review the referee changed his mind, ruling the foul had been outside the box and awarding a free kick instead, along with a red card to the Qatari left back. I don't think either team was entirely happy with that outcome.

Nothing came from the free kick, but the extra man made an uneven match increasingly lopsided, and Canada grabbed a third before half time.

What looked like a fairly innocuous Qatari challenge ten minutes into the second half ended with a Canadian being carried off on a stretcher with a suspected broken leg and a second Qatari taking an early shower. It felt like one of those situations where the referee punishes the outcome rather than the action, but that is often the way.

Qatari heads dropped even further, their challenges got sloppier, and their defence was eventually reduced to walking pace. That Canada only added three more goals will almost feel like a relief.

title: A Win Is a Win (Just About)
body:
Mexico went into this game knowing that a win would earn them top of the group, early qualification to the knockouts, a last 32 game against a third place team, and home field advantage until at least the last 16 should they make it that far. In short, there was plenty to play for.

For much of the match it felt like one of those boxing bouts where neither fighter wants to throw the first punch. The Mexican goal, when it eventually arrived, was a gift from the Korean keeper. Poor communication with his centre back left him flapping at the ball, and Mexico were alert enough to capitalise and poke it into the net.

At that point Mexico appeared to decide they had quite enough goals, thank you, and for much of the remaining time it was South Korea making all the effort to change things, without ever quite finding the end product to do so.

They did have one late chance that threatened to sneak over the line, but the Mexican keeper was just about able to claw it away.

Mexico will be celebrating. Two wins, neither entirely deserved, and a very comfortable path to the knockouts. They will need to find another gear if they're to get past the last 32, but that's a problem for another day.

Korea need a win from their final group game, and will need to be considerably more ruthless with their chances when it comes.

title: Tartan Nightmare
body:
Haiti was always going to be the easiest of Scotland's World Cup fixtures, but even with most teams in this tournament able to progress, regardless of their opening result, they were always going to need something from at least one of their remaining two games.

Morocco, who both won and lost the most recent African Cup of Nations, were their next opponents and a considerably sterner test.

It was barely two minutes in when Morocco went one up, and Scotland were immediately left searching for a plan B.

Unfortunately, plan B turned out to be giving Morocco enormous amounts of space and hoping they'd waste their chances.

It was injury time before Scotland recovered from the early shock and managed to register anything that even resembled a chance.
The second half saw Scotland get more of the ball as they chased the equaliser that would have earned them the point they needed, but they were never quite good enough. Morocco continued to spurn chances, but in the end it didn't matter.

Scotland looked better in the second half than their dreadful first, but they will struggle to get anything from even the sluggish Brazil we saw last week. There is a chance they scrape through to the knockouts on three points, but it's not a hook I'd hang all my hopes on.

title: Comfortable, Then Less So
body:
By the time the US scored ten minutes in they had already fashioned several chances, while Australia were struggling to get out of the starting blocks. As the half progressed so did the American dominance, though there were signs from the Socceroos that they would at least put up a fight.

A second goal close to half time felt like it should wrap up the win and potentially spur the US on to more. The goal, bundled in from a looping deflection, was originally flagged offside but overturned on review. (See footer for my thoughts on the offside rule, which I have many thoughts about.)

The second half felt like a different game entirely. The US lost some of their earlier confidence while Australia saw more of the ball and more of the chances. Defensive mistakes and sustained pressure threatened to give Australia a route back into the match, but the home nation held firm and took their second win of the tournament.
footer:
A note on the offside rule

Now, this isn't really the place to air my views on the offside rule. Then again, it's my World Cup blog, so why not. Apologies in advance. I'll try to keep it light.
I have real issues with how "interfering with play" is interpreted. Firstly, if an attacking player can be deemed not to be interfering with play, why can't the same logic apply to defenders?

Secondly, and more pressingly if you happen to be the offside player: if you're running towards the keeper shoulder to shoulder with the player who will actually score the goal (and who was onside), you are absolutely interfering with play. The goalkeeper is having to work out which of you to prioritise. The defender is being blocked. How, precisely, is the second attacker not interfering with play?

Sorry. My editor has asked me to move on. If you'd like to continue this conversation, feel free to find me during a hydration break in the kitchen.
